naturalMeaning: Not just 'sauna' in translation — баня is its own institution: a wood- or stove-heated steam room used with a venik whisk and cold plunges, woven into weekly routine and hospitality rather than treated as a spa extra.

examples.text: Мы идём в баню в субботу.
examples.romaji: My idyóm v bányu v subbótu.
examples.translation: We're going to the banya on Saturday.

naturalMeaning: Used to distinguish the wet-steam, venik-whisking tradition from a Finnish сауна (dry heat, no whisking) when the difference needs spelling out.
The hottest inner room of the banya, built around the каменка stove — this is where the venik whisking and steam-throwing happen, as opposed to the предбанник where you undress and rest.

examples.translation: Let's go into the steam room!

translation: changing room / anteroom of the banya
naturalMeaning: The cooler outer room where you undress, leave your things, and sit to cool down and rest between rounds in the парная.
In bigger public banyas, a separate room with basins and showers for actually washing — distinct from the парная, where you steam and whisk but don't scrub.

A bound bundle of birch, oak, or other leafy branches, soaked to soften, then used to fan hot air and gently strike the skin — the central tool and ritual of a proper banya session, not a cleaning implement.
The most common venik — softer leaves, a light honey-like scent, and a reputation for being gentler on the skin than oak.
Sturdier, broader leaves than birch — holds up to more rounds of whisking and is often preferred for oily or problem skin; see the culture notes for the full birch-vs-oak debate.
A dry venik is soaked in hot, then near-boiling, water for several minutes to soften the leaves and release their scent before it's ever used on skin — using it dry would just hurt.

translation: to steam oneself / take a steam session
naturalMeaning: The general verb for the whole activity of being in the hot парная — sitting in the heat, being whisked, sweating it out — not just 'to steam' in a culinary sense.

examples.translation: Do you like taking a steam?

translation: to whisk oneself with a venik
naturalMeaning: The specific ritual of using the venik on the body in the steam room, usually done for you by a friend or a professional банщик rather than alone.

translation: to fan/waft hot air with the venik
naturalMeaning: The gentler opening move before any striking — fanning the venik just above the skin to push hot steam down onto the body and warm it up gradually.
A thick felt hat worn in the парная to protect the head and hair from the intense dry heat near the ceiling — without one, your head can overheat well before the rest of your body does.

translation: to add steam (pour water on the hot stones)
naturalMeaning: Ladling water — sometimes with beer, herbs, or essential oil mixed in — onto the red-hot stones of the каменка to send a fresh wave of steam through the room. Always ask before doing it for others; too much too fast can drive everyone out.
The wood- or electric-heated stove piled with stones at its top — pouring water onto these stones is what generates the banya's steam.

translation: the wooden bench/shelf in the steam room
naturalMeaning: Steam rises, so the полок is built in tiers — the top shelf is the hottest and reserved for the most heat-tolerant bathers, the lower ones are milder.
Distinct from пар (steam) — жар is the dry, radiant heat itself, the kind people say they need to 'endure' (терпеть жар).

translation: sheet (worn wrapped around the body in the banya)
naturalMeaning: The standard thing to wrap yourself in between the предбанник and парная, rather than street clothes or (usually) nothing — see the culture notes on nudity norms.
A small wooden or plastic tub used to carry and pour water over yourself, especially where there's no shower — filled at a tap and dumped over the head or shoulders.
The long-handled scoop used to pour water from the шайка over the hot stones of the каменка — the tool of поддать пару.
A few drops mixed into the water before it's poured on the stones — eucalyptus and mint are common choices — to scent the steam that fills the парная.

translation: ice hole (cut in a frozen river, lake, or pond)
naturalMeaning: The classic dead-of-winter cold plunge — a hole cut through the ice next to a countryside banya, so bathers can run straight from the hottest room into freezing water and back again.

examples.translation: Are you ready to jump into the ice hole?

translation: to douse oneself with cold water
naturalMeaning: The milder, everyday version of the ice-hole plunge — a bucket or shower of cold water right after the steam room, said to close the pores and shock the body awake.
The urban, year-round equivalent of the прорубь — an unheated small pool built right next to the parnaya, for the same hot-cold cycle without needing a frozen lake nearby.

Said to someone right after they finish steaming — a fixed, almost ceremonial line wishing them a good, 'light' (easy, healthy) steam, roughly like saying 'enjoy your bath' but far more ritualized. See the culture notes for when and how it's used.
The traditional way to close out a banya session — sitting, rehydrating, and talking over hot tea (sometimes with honey or jam) once the steaming and plunging are done.
A mildly fizzy, barely alcoholic drink often brought out after the banya alongside tea — valued for rehydrating without the dehydrating effect of alcohol.
A bathhouse open to any paying visitor, usually with separate men's and women's sections or scheduled hours — the classic urban banya experience, as opposed to a private family one.

A banya belonging to a family's dacha (country house) or rented for a group — the more common way many Russians actually experience banya culture, built around close friends and relatives rather than strangers.
Used to specifically mean the Finnish-style dry-heat room with no venik whisking and no steam-throwing ritual — Russians distinguish it clearly from баня even though both are hot rooms.
A staff member at bigger public banyas who whisks and lightly massages guests with the venik for a fee — a real, trained trade, not an amateur favor.

translation: to go into the steam room
to step out to cool down
naturalMeaning: The other half of the rhythm — nobody stays in the парная continuously; sessions are short rounds of heat followed by cooling off in the предбанник or outside.

translation: to sit for a while in the steam room

naturalMeaning: Historically Saturday, when families without their own bath would go to the public banya to properly wash for the week — the phrase survives even now that most homes have showers.
